arguments.file_text:
# Grafana v11.4.0 — Engineering Onboarding Guide

This document is a practical orientation for engineers joining the Grafana codebase. It covers the six areas you need to understand before making your first meaningful contribution: server bootstrap, core backend packages, datasource plugin architecture, dashboard provisioning, the frontend build system, and how to add a new panel plugin.

---

## Table of Contents

1. [Repository Layout](#1-repository-layout)
2. [Main Entry Points and Server Bootstrap](#2-main-entry-points-and-server-bootstrap)
3. [Core Backend Packages](#3-core-backend-packages)
4. [Datasource Plugin Architecture](#4-datasource-plugin-architecture)
5. [Dashboard Provisioning Pipeline](#5-dashboard-provisioning-pipeline)
6. [Frontend Build System](#6-frontend-build-system)
7. [Adding a New Panel Plugin](#7-adding-a-new-panel-plugin)

---

## 1. Repository Layout

```
grafana/
├── pkg/                        # All Go backend code
│   ├── cmd/grafana/            # Primary CLI entry point
│   ├── cmd/grafana-server/     # Legacy server entry point
│   ├── server/                 # Server lifecycle + Google Wire DI
│   ├── api/                    # HTTP routing and handlers
│   ├── registry/               # BackgroundService interfaces
│   ├── plugins/                # Plugin system (manager, backendplugin, repo)
│   ├── tsdb/                   # Core datasource backends (Prometheus, Loki, …)
│   ├── services/               # All domain services
│   │   ├── authn/              # Authentication
│   │   ├── dashboards/         # Dashboard CRUD service
│   │   ├── datasources/        # Datasource CRUD service
│   │   ├── ngalert/            # Alerting engine
│   │   ├── pluginsintegration/ # Plugin wiring (Wire sets, pipeline)
│   │   └── provisioning/       # File-based provisioning
│   └── setting/                # Configuration (grafana.ini parsing)
├── public/
│   ├── app/                    # TypeScript/React frontend application
│   │   ├── app.ts              # Frontend bootstrap
│   │   ├── AppWrapper.tsx      # React root component
│   │   ├── core/               # Shared utilities, services, components
│   │   ├── features/           # Feature-area modules (dashboards, explore, …)
│   │   └── plugins/
│   │       ├── panel/          # Built-in panel plugins (timeseries, table, …)
│   │       └── datasource/     # Built-in datasource frontend plugins
│   └── sass/                   # Global SCSS themes
├── packages/                   # Published npm packages (@grafana/data, ui, runtime, …)
├── plugins-bundled/            # Bundled (non-core) plugins
├── scripts/webpack/            # Webpack configuration
└── e2e/                        # End-to-end test suites (Cypress + Playwright)
```

---

## 2. Main Entry Points and Server Bootstrap

### 2.1 Binary Entry Points

**Primary binary** — `pkg/cmd/grafana/main.go`

```
main()
  └─ cmd.RunGrafanaCmd()          // pkg/cmd/grafana/main.go
       └─ MainApp()               // builds *cli.App (github.com/urfave/cli/v2)
            ├─ ServerCommand      // "grafana server"
            └─ CLICommand         // "grafana cli"
```

**Legacy binary** — `pkg/cmd/grafana-server/main.go`

A thin shim that calls `cmd.RunGrafanaCmd("server")`. It exists for backward compatibility with init scripts that invoke `grafana-server` directly.

### 2.2 Server Lifecycle

The server is constructed and run in `pkg/server/server.go`:

```
server.New(cfg, …)
  └─ newServer()                  // allocates Server struct
       └─ s.Init()                // calls Init() on every registered service
            └─ s.Run()            // starts all BackgroundService goroutines
                 └─ s.Shutdown()  // graceful drain on SIGTERM/SIGINT
```

The `Server` struct holds a slice of `registry.BackgroundService` implementations. Each service's `Run(ctx)` goroutine is started concurrently; the server waits for all of them to exit before returning.

### 2.3 Dependency Injection with Google Wire

All service wiring lives in `pkg/server/wire.go`. Grafana uses [Google Wire](https://github.com/google/wire) for compile-time dependency injection. The key provider sets are:

| Wire set | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `wireBasicSet` | Minimal set for lightweight commands |
| `wireSet` | Full production server |
| `wireCLISet` | CLI-only commands |
| `wireTestSet` | Integration test overrides |

The top-level initializers generated by Wire are:

```go
Initialize()              // production server
InitializeForTest()       // test server
InitializeForCLI()        // CLI commands
InitializeModuleServer()  // modular server mode
InitializeAPIServerFactory()
```

Wire reads the `ProvideXxx` constructor functions across all packages and generates the wiring code in `wire_gen.go`. When you add a new service, you add its `ProvideXxx` function to the appropriate Wire set in `wire.go`.

### 2.4 HTTP Server

`pkg/api/http_server.go` defines `HTTPServer`, which aggregates every service needed to handle HTTP requests (100+ injected fields). Route registration happens in `pkg/api/api.go` via `registerRoutes()`, which mounts handlers for the REST API, plugin proxy, live streaming, and static assets.

---

## 3. Core Backend Packages

### 3.1 Service Registry — `pkg/registry`

```go
// BackgroundService is implemented by any service that needs a long-running goroutine.
type BackgroundService interface {
    Run(ctx context.Context) error
}

// CanBeDisabled lets a service opt out at runtime based on configuration.
type CanBeDisabled interface {
    IsDisabled() bool
}
```

Every service that needs to run continuously (alerting scheduler, provisioning poller, live streaming, etc.) implements `BackgroundService`. The server collects all of them via Wire and starts them in `s.Run()`.

### 3.2 Configuration — `pkg/setting`

`setting.Cfg` is the central configuration struct populated from `grafana.ini` (and environment variable overrides). It is injected everywhere via Wire. Key fields include database connection strings, plugin paths, provisioning paths, feature toggles, and security settings.

### 3.3 Infrastructure — `pkg/infra`

| Sub-package | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| `pkg/infra/db` | Database abstraction (SQLite / MySQL / PostgreSQL via `xorm`) |
| `pkg/infra/log` | Structured logger (wraps `go-kit/log`) |
| `pkg/infra/metrics` | Prometheus metrics registration |
| `pkg/infra/tracing` | OpenTelemetry tracing |
| `pkg/infra/kvstore` | Key-value store abstraction |

### 3.4 Domain Services — `pkg/services`

Each domain area lives in its own package under `pkg/services/`:

| Package | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| `authn` | Authentication pipeline — `authn.go` defines the `Service` interface; `authnimpl/` holds the implementation; `clients/` contains individual auth clients (API key, JWT, LDAP, OAuth, …) |
| `dashboards` | Dashboard CRUD, `DashboardService` and `DashboardProvisioningService` interfaces |
| `datasources` | Datasource CRUD, `DataSourceService` interface |
| `folder` | Folder hierarchy management |
| `ngalert` | Alerting engine — scheduler, evaluator, notifier, state machine, provisioning |
| `accesscontrol` | Role-based access control (RBAC) |
| `featuremgmt` | Feature toggle evaluation |
| `quota` | Resource quota enforcement |
| `secrets` | Secret encryption/decryption |
| `org` | Multi-org management |

### 3.5 Plugin System — `pkg/plugins`

The plugin system is described in detail in [Section 4](#4-datasource-plugin-architecture). The top-level `pkg/plugins/plugins.go` defines the `Plugin` struct and the four plugin types:

```go
const (
    TypeDatasource      Type = "datasource"
    TypePanel           Type = "panel"
    TypeApp             Type = "app"
    TypeRenderer        Type = "renderer"
    TypeSecretsManager  Type = "secretsmanager"
)
```

And four plugin classes:

```go
const (
    ClassCore     Class = "core"     // compiled into the binary (pkg/tsdb/*)
    ClassBundled  Class = "bundled"  // shipped with Grafana, loaded from disk
    ClassExternal Class = "external" // installed by users
    ClassCDN      Class = "cdn"      // loaded from Grafana CDN
)
```

---

## 4. Datasource Plugin Architecture

Grafana datasource plugins have two halves: a **backend** (Go) that executes queries and a **frontend** (TypeScript) that renders the query editor. The two halves are linked by a shared plugin ID declared in `plugin.json`.

### 4.1 Plugin Lifecycle Pipeline

Plugin loading is orchestrated through a five-stage pipeline defined in `pkg/services/pluginsintegration/`:

```
Discovery  →  Bootstrap  →  Validation  →  Initialization  →  (Termination)
   ↓              ↓              ↓                ↓
finder.go    bootstrap/     validation/      initialization/
             pipeline       pipeline         pipeline
```

1. **Discovery** (`pipeline/discovery`) — scans configured plugin directories and the CDN for `plugin.json` manifests.
2. **Bootstrap** (`pipeline/bootstrap`) — reads and parses each `plugin.json` into a `plugins.Plugin` struct.
3. **Validation** (`pipeline/validation`) — verifies plugin signatures; unsigned plugins are rejected in production unless explicitly allowed.
4. **Initialization** (`pipeline/initialization`) — starts the backend process (gRPC or in-process) and registers the plugin in the registry.
5. **Termination** (`pipeline/termination`) — gracefully stops backend processes on unload.

### 4.2 Core Datasource Backends

Core datasources (Prometheus, Loki, Elasticsearch, etc.) are compiled directly into the Grafana binary. They live in `pkg/tsdb/` and are registered in `pkg/plugins/backendplugin/coreplugin/registry.go`:

```go
// ProvideCoreRegistry wires every core datasource service into a Registry.
func ProvideCoreRegistry(tracer, am, cw, cm, es, …) *Registry {
    return NewRegistry(map[string]backendplugin.PluginFactoryFunc{
        "cloudwatch":                      asBackendPlugin(cw.Executor),
        "prometheus":                      asBackendPlugin(pr),
        "loki":                            asBackendPlugin(lk),
        "grafana-postgresql-datasource":   asBackendPlugin(pg),
        // … 17 total core datasources
    })
}
```

`asBackendPlugin` wraps any service that implements one or more of the SDK handler interfaces (`QueryDataHandler`, `CallResourceHandler`, `CheckHealthHandler`, `StreamHandler`) into a `backendplugin.PluginFactoryFunc`.

### 4.3 External Datasource Backends (gRPC)

External datasource plugins run as child processes and communicate over gRPC using [hashicorp/go-plugin](https://github.com/hashicorp/go-plugin). The implementation is in `pkg/plugins/backendplugin/grpcplugin/grpc_plugin.go`:

```
Grafana process                    Plugin process
─────────────────                  ─────────────────
grpcPlugin.Start()
  └─ plugin.NewClient()  ──gRPC──► plugin binary
       └─ newClientV2()            implements backend.QueryDataHandler
                                              backend.CheckHealthHandler
                                              backend.CallResourceHandler
                                              backend.StreamHandler
```

The `grpcPlugin` struct implements the full `backendplugin.Plugin` interface, delegating every call (`QueryData`, `CheckHealth`, `CallResource`, `SubscribeStream`, `RunStream`, …) to the remote process via `ClientV2`.

### 4.4 Backend SDK Interfaces

All datasource backends (core and external) implement interfaces from `github.com/grafana/grafana-plugin-sdk-go/backend`:

```go
type QueryDataHandler interface {
    QueryData(ctx context.Context, req *QueryDataRequest) (*QueryDataResponse, error)
}
type CheckHealthHandler interface {
    CheckHealth(ctx context.Context, req *CheckHealthRequest) (*CheckHealthResult, error)
}
type CallResourceHandler interface {
    CallResource(ctx context.Context, req *CallResourceRequest, sender CallResourceResponseSender) error
}
type StreamHandler interface {
    SubscribeStream(ctx context.Context, req *SubscribeStreamRequest) (*SubscribeStreamResponse, error)
    RunStream(ctx context.Context, req *RunStreamRequest, sender *StreamSender) error
    PublishStream(ctx context.Context, req *PublishStreamRequest) (*PublishStreamResponse, error)
}
```

### 4.5 Datasource Proxy

HTTP requests from the browser to a datasource's own HTTP API are routed through `pkg/services/datasourceproxy/datasourceproxy.go`. The proxy authenticates the request, resolves the datasource URL from the database, and forwards the request — adding any configured auth headers (Basic, Bearer, etc.).

### 4.6 Frontend Datasource Plugin

Each datasource has a TypeScript module under `public/app/plugins/datasource/<id>/`. The module exports a class extending `DataSourceApi` from `@grafana/data` and a `QueryEditor` React component. The plugin is loaded at runtime via SystemJS.

---

## 5. Dashboard Provisioning Pipeline

Provisioning lets operators manage Grafana resources (dashboards, datasources, plugins, alerting) as code — YAML files on disk that Grafana reads at startup and polls for changes.

### 5.1 Top-Level Orchestrator

`pkg/services/provisioning/provisioning.go` — `ProvisioningServiceImpl` — is a `BackgroundService` that owns all four provisioning domains:

```go
type ProvisioningServiceImpl struct {
    newDashboardProvisioner dashboards.DashboardProvisionerFactory
    provisionDatasources    func(ctx, path, …) error
    provisionPlugins        func(ctx, path, …) error
    provisionAlerting       func(ctx, …) error
    // …
}
```

On startup, `Run()` calls each domain's `Provision()` function sequentially, then starts background pollers.

### 5.2 Dashboard Provisioning in Detail

The dashboard provisioning pipeline has four components:

```
conf/provisioning/dashboards/*.yaml
         │
         ▼
  configReader.readConfig()          // pkg/services/provisioning/dashboards/config_reader.go
         │  parses YAML (v0 or v1 apiVersion)
         │  produces []*config
         ▼
  getFileReaders()                   // dashboard.go
         │  creates one FileReader per provider config
         ▼
  Provisioner.Provision()            // dashboard.go
         │  calls reader.walkDisk(ctx) for each FileReader
         │  calls duplicateValidator.validate()
         ▼
  FileReader.walkDisk()              // file_reader.go
         │  walks the configured path recursively
         │  for each .json file: reads, parses, upserts via DashboardProvisioningService
         ▼
  DashboardProvisioningService       // pkg/services/dashboards (database layer)
```

**Polling** — `Provisioner.PollChanges(ctx)` launches one goroutine per `FileReader`. Each goroutine calls `walkDisk` on the interval specified by `updateIntervalSeconds` in the provider config (default: 10 s).

**Orphan cleanup** — `CleanUpOrphanedDashboards` deletes database rows for dashboards whose source file no longer exists.

### 5.3 Provider Config Schema (v1)

```yaml
# conf/provisioning/dashboards/my-provider.yaml
apiVersion: 1
providers:
  - name: my-dashboards          # unique name
    type: file                   # only "file" is supported
    orgId: 1
    folder: MyFolder             # target folder in Grafana UI
    folderUid: ""
    disableDeletion: false       # keep DB rows when file is deleted
    updateIntervalSeconds: 30
    allowUiUpdates: false        # prevent UI edits of provisioned dashboards
    options:
      path: /var/lib/grafana/dashboards
      foldersFromFilesStructure: false  # mirror directory structure as folders
```

### 5.4 Other Provisioning Domains

| Domain | Config path | Package |
|---|---|---|
| Datasources | `conf/provisioning/datasources/` | `pkg/services/provisioning/datasources` |
| Plugins | `conf/provisioning/plugins/` | `pkg/services/provisioning/plugins` |
| Alerting | `conf/provisioning/alerting/` | `pkg/services/provisioning/alerting` |

Each domain follows the same pattern: a `configReader` parses YAML, a `Provision` function applies the config to the database, and a background goroutine polls for changes.

---

## 6. Frontend Build System

### 6.1 Technology Stack

| Tool | Role |
|---|---|
| **Webpack 5** | Module bundler |
| **esbuild-loader** | TypeScript/TSX transpilation (replaces ts-loader for speed) |
| **Sass + PostCSS** | CSS preprocessing |
| **Nx** | Monorepo task orchestration |
| **Yarn 4 (Berry)** | Package manager with workspaces |
| **Jest** | Unit testing |
| **Cypress / Playwright** | End-to-end testing |

### 6.2 Webpack Configuration

The Webpack config is split across three files in `scripts/webpack/`:

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `webpack.common.js` | Shared config: entry points, output, module resolution, loaders |
| `webpack.dev.js` | Development: `webpack-dev-server`, source maps, React Fast Refresh |
| `webpack.prod.js` | Production: esbuild minification, content-hashed filenames, CSS extraction |

**Entry points** (from `webpack.common.js`):

```js
entry: {
  app:     './public/app/index.ts',   // main application bundle
  swagger: './public/swagger/index.tsx',
}
```

**Output** — all bundles land in `public/build/` with content-hashed filenames (`app.[contenthash].js`). Two manifest files are emitted:
- `webpack-assets-manifest.json` — includes SRI integrity hashes (used by the Go server to inject `<script>` tags)
- `manifest.json` — simpler filename map

**Module resolution** — `public/` is added to the resolver's `modules` list, enabling bare imports like `import config from 'app/core/config'`.

### 6.3 npm Workspace Packages

The `packages/` directory contains the published `@grafana/*` npm packages. They are consumed by the main app as `workspace:*` dependencies:

| Package | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `@grafana/data` | Core data types: `DataFrame`, `PanelPlugin`, `DataSourceApi`, field config, transformers |
| `@grafana/runtime` | Runtime singletons: `BackendSrv`, `DataSourceSrv`, `locationService`, plugin extension hooks |
| `@grafana/ui` | React component library: `Button`, `Select`, `Table`, `PanelChrome`, etc. |
| `@grafana/schema` | CUE-generated TypeScript types for panel/datasource schemas |
| `@grafana/prometheus` | Shared Prometheus query editor components |
| `@grafana/sql` | Shared SQL query editor components |

Build all packages: `yarn packages:build` (runs `nx run-many -t build --projects='@grafana/*'`).

### 6.4 Frontend Bootstrap Sequence

```
public/app/index.ts
  └─ public/app/app.ts
       ├─ Registers @grafana/runtime singletons
       │    (setBackendSrv, setDataSourceSrv, setLocationSrv, …)
       ├─ Initializes i18n (initializeI18n)
       ├─ Registers standard field config editors and transformers
       ├─ Loads icon cache
       └─ Mounts <AppWrapper /> into #reactRoot via React 18 createRoot()
```

`AppWrapper.tsx` sets up the Redux store, React Router, and the `GrafanaContext` provider before rendering the main application shell.

### 6.5 Common Development Commands

```bash
# Start dev server with hot reload
yarn start

# Production build
yarn build

# Run unit tests in watch mode
yarn test

# Type-check everything
yarn typecheck

# Lint TypeScript
yarn lint:ts

# Build all @grafana/* packages
yarn packages:build

# Build all bundled panel plugins
yarn plugin:build
```

---

## 7. Adding a New Panel Plugin

Panel plugins are pure frontend — they require no Go code. A panel plugin consists of a `plugin.json` manifest, a `module.tsx` entry point, and a React component.

### 7.1 File Structure

Create a new directory under `public/app/plugins/panel/<your-plugin-id>/`:

```
public/app/plugins/panel/my-panel/
├── plugin.json          # Plugin metadata
├── module.tsx           # Plugin registration (must export `plugin`)
├── MyPanel.tsx          # React panel component
├── types.ts             # Options and FieldConfig TypeScript types
├── panelcfg.cue         # (optional) CUE schema for options
└── img/
    └── icon.svg         # Panel icon shown in the panel picker
```

### 7.2 `plugin.json`

```json
{
  "type": "panel",
  "name": "My Panel",
  "id": "my-panel",
  "info": {
    "description": "A short description of what this panel does",
    "author": {
      "name": "Your Name",
      "url": "https://example.com"
    },
    "logos": {
      "small": "img/icon.svg",
      "large": "img/icon.svg"
    }
  }
}
```

The `id` field must be globally unique. For built-in panels it is a simple slug (e.g., `timeseries`, `table`). For external plugins it is conventionally namespaced (e.g., `myorg-mypanel-panel`).

### 7.3 `module.tsx` — Plugin Registration

The file **must** export a named constant `plugin` of type `PanelPlugin`. Grafana's plugin loader looks for this export by convention.

```tsx
import { PanelPlugin } from '@grafana/data';
import { MyPanel } from './MyPanel';

// Minimal panel — no options
export const plugin = new PanelPlugin<Options>(MyPanel);
```

A more complete example with options and field config (modelled on the built-in `timeseries` panel at `public/app/plugins/panel/timeseries/module.tsx`):

```tsx
import { PanelPlugin } from '@grafana/data';
import { MyPanel } from './MyPanel';
import { Options, FieldConfig } from './types';

export const plugin = new PanelPlugin<Options, FieldConfig>(MyPanel)
  // Declare panel-level options (shown in the panel editor sidebar)
  .setPanelOptions((builder) => {
    builder
      .addBooleanSwitch({
        path: 'showLegend',
        name: 'Show legend',
        defaultValue: true,
      })
      .addSelect({
        path: 'colorMode',
        name: 'Color mode',
        defaultValue: 'palette',
        settings: {
          options: [
            { value: 'palette', label: 'Palette' },
            { value: 'fixed', label: 'Fixed color' },
          ],
        },
      });
  })
  // Declare per-field overrides (shown in the Overrides tab)
  .useFieldConfig({
    standardOptions: {
      color: { fixedColorMode: 'fixed' },
    },
    useCustomConfig: (builder) => {
      builder.addNumberInput({
        path: 'lineWidth',
        name: 'Line width',
        defaultValue: 1,
      });
    },
  })
  // Handle migration from a previous panel type
  .setPanelChangeHandler((panel, prevPluginId, prevOptions) => {
    if (prevPluginId === 'graph') {
      return { ...panel, options: { showLegend: true } };
    }
    return panel;
  })
  // Declare what data features the panel supports
  .setDataSupport({ annotations: false, alertStates: false });
```

### 7.4 `MyPanel.tsx` — The React Component

```tsx
import React from 'react';
import { PanelProps } from '@grafana/data';
import { Options } from './types';

// PanelProps<Options> provides: data, options, width, height, timeRange,
// timeZone, onOptionsChange, onFieldConfigChange, replaceVariables, …
export function MyPanel({ data, options, width, height }: PanelProps<Options>) {
  const frame = data.series[0];

  if (!frame) {
    return <div>No data</div>;
  }

  return (
    <div style={{ width, height, overflow: 'auto' }}>
      {options.showLegend && <div className="legend">Legend</div>}
      {/* render your visualization here */}
    </div>
  );
}
```

### 7.5 `types.ts` — Options and FieldConfig

```ts
// Panel-level options (one set per panel instance)
export interface Options {
  showLegend: boolean;
  colorMode: 'palette' | 'fixed';
}

// Per-field config (can be overridden per series/field)
export interface FieldConfig {
  lineWidth?: number;
}
```

### 7.6 Registering the Plugin with the Build System

Built-in panel plugins are automatically discovered by Webpack because `public/app/plugins/panel/` is included in the Yarn workspace glob:

```json
// package.json (root)
"workspaces": {
  "packages": [
    "public/app/plugins/*/*"   // ← picks up your new panel
  ]
}
```

No additional registration step is needed for built-in panels. The plugin loader resolves `module.tsx` at runtime via SystemJS using the plugin ID from `plugin.json`.

For **external** plugins (distributed separately), you would instead use the `@grafana/create-plugin` scaffolding tool and the `@grafana/plugin-configs` Webpack preset from `packages/grafana-plugin-configs/`.

### 7.7 Testing Your Panel

```bash
# Unit tests — create MyPanel.test.tsx alongside MyPanel.tsx
yarn test --testPathPattern=my-panel

# Start the dev server and open Grafana at http://localhost:3000
# Add a new panel to a dashboard and select "My Panel" from the panel picker
yarn start
```

### 7.8 Key `PanelPlugin` Builder Methods Reference

| Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `setPanelOptions(builder => …)` | Declare panel-level option editors |
| `useFieldConfig({ standardOptions, useCustomConfig })` | Declare per-field config editors |
| `setPanelChangeHandler(fn)` | Migrate options when switching from another panel type |
| `setDataSupport({ annotations, alertStates })` | Opt in to annotations/alert state overlays |
| `setSuggestionsSupplier(supplier)` | Provide auto-suggestions in the panel picker |
| `setNoPadding()` | Remove the default panel padding (useful for map/canvas panels) |
| `setSkipDataQuery()` | Panel does not query data (e.g., text, image panels) |

---

## Quick Reference: Key Files

| What you want to understand | File(s) to read |
|---|---|
| How the server starts | `pkg/cmd/grafana/main.go`, `pkg/server/server.go` |
| How services are wired together | `pkg/server/wire.go` |
| How HTTP routes are registered | `pkg/api/api.go`, `pkg/api/http_server.go` |
| How to add a background service | `pkg/registry/registry.go` (implement `BackgroundService`) |
| How core datasources are registered | `pkg/plugins/backendplugin/coreplugin/registry.go` |
| How external plugins communicate | `pkg/plugins/backendplugin/grpcplugin/grpc_plugin.go` |
| How dashboard provisioning works | `pkg/services/provisioning/dashboards/dashboard.go`, `file_reader.go` |
| How the frontend bootstraps | `public/app/app.ts`, `public/app/AppWrapper.tsx` |
| How a panel plugin is structured | `public/app/plugins/panel/timeseries/module.tsx` |
| Webpack entry points and output | `scripts/webpack/webpack.common.js` |
| Published npm packages | `packages/` directory |

# Grafana v11.4.0 — Engineering Onboarding Guide

This document is a practical orientation for engineers joining the Grafana codebase. It covers the six areas you need to understand before making your first meaningful contribution: server bootstrap, core backend packages, datasource plugin architecture, dashboard provisioning, the frontend build system, and how to add a new panel plugin.

---

## Table of Contents

1. [Repository Layout](#1-repository-layout)
2. [Main Entry Points and Server Bootstrap](#2-main-entry-points-and-server-bootstrap)
3. [Core Backend Packages](#3-core-backend-packages)
4. [Datasource Plugin Architecture](#4-datasource-plugin-architecture)
5. [Dashboard Provisioning Pipeline](#5-dashboard-provisioning-pipeline)
6. [Frontend Build System](#6-frontend-build-system)
7. [Adding a New Panel Plugin](#7-adding-a-new-panel-plugin)

---

## 1. Repository Layout

```
grafana/
├── pkg/                        # All Go backend code
│   ├── cmd/grafana/            # Primary CLI entry point
│   ├── cmd/grafana-server/     # Legacy server entry point
│   ├── server/                 # Server lifecycle + Google Wire DI
│   ├── api/                    # HTTP routing and handlers
│   ├── registry/               # BackgroundService interfaces
│   ├── plugins/                # Plugin system (manager, backendplugin, repo)
│   ├── tsdb/                   # Core datasource backends (Prometheus, Loki, …)
│   ├── services/               # All domain services
│   │   ├── authn/              # Authentication
│   │   ├── dashboards/         # Dashboard CRUD service
│   │   ├── datasources/        # Datasource CRUD service
│   │   ├── ngalert/            # Alerting engine
│   │   ├── pluginsintegration/ # Plugin wiring (Wire sets, pipeline)
│   │   └── provisioning/       # File-based provisioning
│   └── setting/                # Configuration (grafana.ini parsing)
├── public/
│   ├── app/                    # TypeScript/React frontend application
│   │   ├── app.ts              # Frontend bootstrap
│   │   ├── AppWrapper.tsx      # React root component
│   │   ├── core/               # Shared utilities, services, components
│   │   ├── features/           # Feature-area modules (dashboards, explore, …)
│   │   └── plugins/
│   │       ├── panel/          # Built-in panel plugins (timeseries, table, …)
│   │       └── datasource/     # Built-in datasource frontend plugins
│   └── sass/                   # Global SCSS themes
├── packages/                   # Published npm packages (@grafana/data, ui, runtime, …)
├── plugins-bundled/            # Bundled (non-core) plugins
├── scripts/webpack/            # Webpack configuration
└── e2e/                        # End-to-end test suites (Cypress + Playwright)
```

---

## 2. Main Entry Points and Server Bootstrap

### 2.1 Binary Entry Points

**Primary binary** — `pkg/cmd/grafana/main.go`

```
main()
  └─ cmd.RunGrafanaCmd()          // pkg/cmd/grafana/main.go
       └─ MainApp()               // builds *cli.App (github.com/urfave/cli/v2)
            ├─ ServerCommand      // "grafana server"
            └─ CLICommand         // "grafana cli"
```

**Legacy binary** — `pkg/cmd/grafana-server/main.go`

A thin shim that calls `cmd.RunGrafanaCmd("server")`. It exists for backward compatibility with init scripts that invoke `grafana-server` directly.

### 2.2 Server Lifecycle

The server is constructed and run in `pkg/server/server.go`:

```
server.New(cfg, …)
  └─ newServer()                  // allocates Server struct
       └─ s.Init()                // calls Init() on every registered service
            └─ s.Run()            // starts all BackgroundService goroutines
                 └─ s.Shutdown()  // graceful drain on SIGTERM/SIGINT
```

The `Server` struct holds a slice of `registry.BackgroundService` implementations. Each service's `Run(ctx)` goroutine is started concurrently; the server waits for all of them to exit before returning.

### 2.3 Dependency Injection with Google Wire

All service wiring lives in `pkg/server/wire.go`. Grafana uses [Google Wire](https://github.com/google/wire) for compile-time dependency injection. The key provider sets are:

| Wire set | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `wireBasicSet` | Minimal set for lightweight commands |
| `wireSet` | Full production server |
| `wireCLISet` | CLI-only commands |
| `wireTestSet` | Integration test overrides |

The top-level initializers generated by Wire are:

```go
Initialize()              // production server
InitializeForTest()       // test server
InitializeForCLI()        // CLI commands
InitializeModuleServer()  // modular server mode
InitializeAPIServerFactory()
```

Wire reads the `ProvideXxx` constructor functions across all packages and generates the wiring code in `wire_gen.go`. When you add a new service, you add its `ProvideXxx` function to the appropriate Wire set in `wire.go`.

### 2.4 HTTP Server

`pkg/api/http_server.go` defines `HTTPServer`, which aggregates every service needed to handle HTTP requests (100+ injected fields). Route registration happens in `pkg/api/api.go` via `registerRoutes()`, which mounts handlers for the REST API, plugin proxy, live streaming, and static assets.

---

## 3. Core Backend Packages

### 3.1 Service Registry — `pkg/registry`

```go
// BackgroundService is implemented by any service that needs a long-running goroutine.
type BackgroundService interface {
    Run(ctx context.Context) error
}

// CanBeDisabled lets a service opt out at runtime based on configuration.
type CanBeDisabled interface {
    IsDisabled() bool
}
```

Every service that needs to run continuously (alerting scheduler, provisioning poller, live streaming, etc.) implements `BackgroundService`. The server collects all of them via Wire and starts them in `s.Run()`.

### 3.2 Configuration — `pkg/setting`

`setting.Cfg` is the central configuration struct populated from `grafana.ini` (and environment variable overrides). It is injected everywhere via Wire. Key fields include database connection strings, plugin paths, provisioning paths, feature toggles, and security settings.

### 3.3 Infrastructure — `pkg/infra`

| Sub-package | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| `pkg/infra/db` | Database abstraction (SQLite / MySQL / PostgreSQL via `xorm`) |
| `pkg/infra/log` | Structured logger (wraps `go-kit/log`) |
| `pkg/infra/metrics` | Prometheus metrics registration |
| `pkg/infra/tracing` | OpenTelemetry tracing |
| `pkg/infra/kvstore` | Key-value store abstraction |

### 3.4 Domain Services — `pkg/services`

Each domain area lives in its own package under `pkg/services/`:

| Package | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| `authn` | Authentication pipeline — `authn.go` defines the `Service` interface; `authnimpl/` holds the implementation; `clients/` contains individual auth clients (API key, JWT, LDAP, OAuth, …) |
| `dashboards` | Dashboard CRUD, `DashboardService` and `DashboardProvisioningService` interfaces |
| `datasources` | Datasource CRUD, `DataSourceService` interface |
| `folder` | Folder hierarchy management |
| `ngalert` | Alerting engine — scheduler, evaluator, notifier, state machine, provisioning |
| `accesscontrol` | Role-based access control (RBAC) |
| `featuremgmt` | Feature toggle evaluation |
| `quota` | Resource quota enforcement |
| `secrets` | Secret encryption/decryption |
| `org` | Multi-org management |

### 3.5 Plugin System — `pkg/plugins`

The plugin system is described in detail in [Section 4](#4-datasource-plugin-architecture). The top-level `pkg/plugins/plugins.go` defines the `Plugin` struct and the four plugin types:

```go
const (
    TypeDatasource      Type = "datasource"
    TypePanel           Type = "panel"
    TypeApp             Type = "app"
    TypeRenderer        Type = "renderer"
    TypeSecretsManager  Type = "secretsmanager"
)
```

And four plugin classes:

```go
const (
    ClassCore     Class = "core"     // compiled into the binary (pkg/tsdb/*)
    ClassBundled  Class = "bundled"  // shipped with Grafana, loaded from disk
    ClassExternal Class = "external" // installed by users
    ClassCDN      Class = "cdn"      // loaded from Grafana CDN
)
```

---

## 4. Datasource Plugin Architecture

Grafana datasource plugins have two halves: a **backend** (Go) that executes queries and a **frontend** (TypeScript) that renders the query editor. The two halves are linked by a shared plugin ID declared in `plugin.json`.

### 4.1 Plugin Lifecycle Pipeline

Plugin loading is orchestrated through a five-stage pipeline defined in `pkg/services/pluginsintegration/`:

```
Discovery  →  Bootstrap  →  Validation  →  Initialization  →  (Termination)
   ↓              ↓              ↓                ↓
finder.go    bootstrap/     validation/      initialization/
             pipeline       pipeline         pipeline
```

1. **Discovery** (`pipeline/discovery`) — scans configured plugin directories and the CDN for `plugin.json` manifests.
2. **Bootstrap** (`pipeline/bootstrap`) — reads and parses each `plugin.json` into a `plugins.Plugin` struct.
3. **Validation** (`pipeline/validation`) — verifies plugin signatures; unsigned plugins are rejected in production unless explicitly allowed.
4. **Initialization** (`pipeline/initialization`) — starts the backend process (gRPC or in-process) and registers the plugin in the registry.
5. **Termination** (`pipeline/termination`) — gracefully stops backend processes on unload.

### 4.2 Core Datasource Backends

Core datasources (Prometheus, Loki, Elasticsearch, etc.) are compiled directly into the Grafana binary. They live in `pkg/tsdb/` and are registered in `pkg/plugins/backendplugin/coreplugin/registry.go`:

```go
// ProvideCoreRegistry wires every core datasource service into a Registry.
func ProvideCoreRegistry(tracer, am, cw, cm, es, …) *Registry {
    return NewRegistry(map[string]backendplugin.PluginFactoryFunc{
        "cloudwatch":                      asBackendPlugin(cw.Executor),
        "prometheus":                      asBackendPlugin(pr),
        "loki":                            asBackendPlugin(lk),
        "grafana-postgresql-datasource":   asBackendPlugin(pg),
        // … 17 total core datasources
    })
}
```

`asBackendPlugin` wraps any service that implements one or more of the SDK handler interfaces (`QueryDataHandler`, `CallResourceHandler`, `CheckHealthHandler`, `StreamHandler`) into a `backendplugin.PluginFactoryFunc`.

### 4.3 External Datasource Backends (gRPC)

External datasource plugins run as child processes and communicate over gRPC using [hashicorp/go-plugin](https://github.com/hashicorp/go-plugin). The implementation is in `pkg/plugins/backendplugin/grpcplugin/grpc_plugin.go`:

```
Grafana process                    Plugin process
─────────────────                  ─────────────────
grpcPlugin.Start()
  └─ plugin.NewClient()  ──gRPC──► plugin binary
       └─ newClientV2()            implements backend.QueryDataHandler
                                              backend.CheckHealthHandler
                                              backend.CallResourceHandler
                                              backend.StreamHandler
```

The `grpcPlugin` struct implements the full `backendplugin.Plugin` interface, delegating every call (`QueryData`, `CheckHealth`, `CallResource`, `SubscribeStream`, `RunStream`, …) to the remote process via `ClientV2`.

### 4.4 Backend SDK Interfaces

All datasource backends (core and external) implement interfaces from `github.com/grafana/grafana-plugin-sdk-go/backend`:

```go
type QueryDataHandler interface {
    QueryData(ctx context.Context, req *QueryDataRequest) (*QueryDataResponse, error)
}
type CheckHealthHandler interface {
    CheckHealth(ctx context.Context, req *CheckHealthRequest) (*CheckHealthResult, error)
}
type CallResourceHandler interface {
    CallResource(ctx context.Context, req *CallResourceRequest, sender CallResourceResponseSender) error
}
type StreamHandler interface {
    SubscribeStream(ctx context.Context, req *SubscribeStreamRequest) (*SubscribeStreamResponse, error)
    RunStream(ctx context.Context, req *RunStreamRequest, sender *StreamSender) error
    PublishStream(ctx context.Context, req *PublishStreamRequest) (*PublishStreamResponse, error)
}
```

### 4.5 Datasource Proxy

HTTP requests from the browser to a datasource's own HTTP API are routed through `pkg/services/datasourceproxy/datasourceproxy.go`. The proxy authenticates the request, resolves the datasource URL from the database, and forwards the request — adding any configured auth headers (Basic, Bearer, etc.).

### 4.6 Frontend Datasource Plugin

Each datasource has a TypeScript module under `public/app/plugins/datasource/<id>/`. The module exports a class extending `DataSourceApi` from `@grafana/data` and a `QueryEditor` React component. The plugin is loaded at runtime via SystemJS.

---

## 5. Dashboard Provisioning Pipeline

Provisioning lets operators manage Grafana resources (dashboards, datasources, plugins, alerting) as code — YAML files on disk that Grafana reads at startup and polls for changes.

### 5.1 Top-Level Orchestrator

`pkg/services/provisioning/provisioning.go` — `ProvisioningServiceImpl` — is a `BackgroundService` that owns all four provisioning domains:

```go
type ProvisioningServiceImpl struct {
    newDashboardProvisioner dashboards.DashboardProvisionerFactory
    provisionDatasources    func(ctx, path, …) error
    provisionPlugins        func(ctx, path, …) error
    provisionAlerting       func(ctx, …) error
    // …
}
```

On startup, `Run()` calls each domain's `Provision()` function sequentially, then starts background pollers.

### 5.2 Dashboard Provisioning in Detail

The dashboard provisioning pipeline has four components:

```
conf/provisioning/dashboards/*.yaml
         │
         ▼
  configReader.readConfig()          // pkg/services/provisioning/dashboards/config_reader.go
         │  parses YAML (v0 or v1 apiVersion)
         │  produces []*config
         ▼
  getFileReaders()                   // dashboard.go
         │  creates one FileReader per provider config
         ▼
  Provisioner.Provision()            // dashboard.go
         │  calls reader.walkDisk(ctx) for each FileReader
         │  calls duplicateValidator.validate()
         ▼
  FileReader.walkDisk()              // file_reader.go
         │  walks the configured path recursively
         │  for each .json file: reads, parses, upserts via DashboardProvisioningService
         ▼
  DashboardProvisioningService       // pkg/services/dashboards (database layer)
```

**Polling** — `Provisioner.PollChanges(ctx)` launches one goroutine per `FileReader`. Each goroutine calls `walkDisk` on the interval specified by `updateIntervalSeconds` in the provider config (default: 10 s).

**Orphan cleanup** — `CleanUpOrphanedDashboards` deletes database rows for dashboards whose source file no longer exists.

### 5.3 Provider Config Schema (v1)

```yaml
# conf/provisioning/dashboards/my-provider.yaml
apiVersion: 1
providers:
  - name: my-dashboards          # unique name
    type: file                   # only "file" is supported
    orgId: 1
    folder: MyFolder             # target folder in Grafana UI
    folderUid: ""
    disableDeletion: false       # keep DB rows when file is deleted
    updateIntervalSeconds: 30
    allowUiUpdates: false        # prevent UI edits of provisioned dashboards
    options:
      path: /var/lib/grafana/dashboards
      foldersFromFilesStructure: false  # mirror directory structure as folders
```

### 5.4 Other Provisioning Domains

| Domain | Config path | Package |
|---|---|---|
| Datasources | `conf/provisioning/datasources/` | `pkg/services/provisioning/datasources` |
| Plugins | `conf/provisioning/plugins/` | `pkg/services/provisioning/plugins` |
| Alerting | `conf/provisioning/alerting/` | `pkg/services/provisioning/alerting` |

Each domain follows the same pattern: a `configReader` parses YAML, a `Provision` function applies the config to the database, and a background goroutine polls for changes.

---

## 6. Frontend Build System

### 6.1 Technology Stack

| Tool | Role |
|---|---|
| **Webpack 5** | Module bundler |
| **esbuild-loader** | TypeScript/TSX transpilation (replaces ts-loader for speed) |
| **Sass + PostCSS** | CSS preprocessing |
| **Nx** | Monorepo task orchestration |
| **Yarn 4 (Berry)** | Package manager with workspaces |
| **Jest** | Unit testing |
| **Cypress / Playwright** | End-to-end testing |

### 6.2 Webpack Configuration

The Webpack config is split across three files in `scripts/webpack/`:

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `webpack.common.js` | Shared config: entry points, output, module resolution, loaders |
| `webpack.dev.js` | Development: `webpack-dev-server`, source maps, React Fast Refresh |
| `webpack.prod.js` | Production: esbuild minification, content-hashed filenames, CSS extraction |

**Entry points** (from `webpack.common.js`):

```js
entry: {
  app:     './public/app/index.ts',   // main application bundle
  swagger: './public/swagger/index.tsx',
}
```

**Output** — all bundles land in `public/build/` with content-hashed filenames (`app.[contenthash].js`). Two manifest files are emitted:
- `webpack-assets-manifest.json` — includes SRI integrity hashes (used by the Go server to inject `<script>` tags)
- `manifest.json` — simpler filename map

**Module resolution** — `public/` is added to the resolver's `modules` list, enabling bare imports like `import config from 'app/core/config'`.

### 6.3 npm Workspace Packages

The `packages/` directory contains the published `@grafana/*` npm packages. They are consumed by the main app as `workspace:*` dependencies:

| Package | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `@grafana/data` | Core data types: `DataFrame`, `PanelPlugin`, `DataSourceApi`, field config, transformers |
| `@grafana/runtime` | Runtime singletons: `BackendSrv`, `DataSourceSrv`, `locationService`, plugin extension hooks |
| `@grafana/ui` | React component library: `Button`, `Select`, `Table`, `PanelChrome`, etc. |
| `@grafana/schema` | CUE-generated TypeScript types for panel/datasource schemas |
| `@grafana/prometheus` | Shared Prometheus query editor components |
| `@grafana/sql` | Shared SQL query editor components |

Build all packages: `yarn packages:build` (runs `nx run-many -t build --projects='@grafana/*'`).

### 6.4 Frontend Bootstrap Sequence

```
public/app/index.ts
  └─ public/app/app.ts
       ├─ Registers @grafana/runtime singletons
       │    (setBackendSrv, setDataSourceSrv, setLocationSrv, …)
       ├─ Initializes i18n (initializeI18n)
       ├─ Registers standard field config editors and transformers
       ├─ Loads icon cache
       └─ Mounts <AppWrapper /> into #reactRoot via React 18 createRoot()
```

`AppWrapper.tsx` sets up the Redux store, React Router, and the `GrafanaContext` provider before rendering the main application shell.

### 6.5 Common Development Commands

```bash
# Start dev server with hot reload
yarn start

# Production build
yarn build

# Run unit tests in watch mode
yarn test

# Type-check everything
yarn typecheck

# Lint TypeScript
yarn lint:ts

# Build all @grafana/* packages
yarn packages:build

# Build all bundled panel plugins
yarn plugin:build
```

---

## 7. Adding a New Panel Plugin

Panel plugins are pure frontend — they require no Go code. A panel plugin consists of a `plugin.json` manifest, a `module.tsx` entry point, and a React component.

### 7.1 File Structure

Create a new directory under `public/app/plugins/panel/<your-plugin-id>/`:

```
public/app/plugins/panel/my-panel/
├── plugin.json          # Plugin metadata
├── module.tsx           # Plugin registration (must export `plugin`)
├── MyPanel.tsx          # React panel component
├── types.ts             # Options and FieldConfig TypeScript types
├── panelcfg.cue         # (optional) CUE schema for options
└── img/
    └── icon.svg         # Panel icon shown in the panel picker
```

### 7.2 `plugin.json`

```json
{
  "type": "panel",
  "name": "My Panel",
  "id": "my-panel",
  "info": {
    "description": "A short description of what this panel does",
    "author": {
      "name": "Your Name",
      "url": "https://example.com"
    },
    "logos": {
      "small": "img/icon.svg",
      "large": "img/icon.svg"
    }
  }
}
```

The `id` field must be globally unique. For built-in panels it is a simple slug (e.g., `timeseries`, `table`). For external plugins it is conventionally namespaced (e.g., `myorg-mypanel-panel`).

### 7.3 `module.tsx` — Plugin Registration

The file **must** export a named constant `plugin` of type `PanelPlugin`. Grafana's plugin loader looks for this export by convention.

```tsx
import { PanelPlugin } from '@grafana/data';
import { MyPanel } from './MyPanel';

// Minimal panel — no options
export const plugin = new PanelPlugin<Options>(MyPanel);
```

A more complete example with options and field config (modelled on the built-in `timeseries` panel at `public/app/plugins/panel/timeseries/module.tsx`):

```tsx
import { PanelPlugin } from '@grafana/data';
import { MyPanel } from './MyPanel';
import { Options, FieldConfig } from './types';

export const plugin = new PanelPlugin<Options, FieldConfig>(MyPanel)
  // Declare panel-level options (shown in the panel editor sidebar)
  .setPanelOptions((builder) => {
    builder
      .addBooleanSwitch({
        path: 'showLegend',
        name: 'Show legend',
        defaultValue: true,
      })
      .addSelect({
        path: 'colorMode',
        name: 'Color mode',
        defaultValue: 'palette',
        settings: {
          options: [
            { value: 'palette', label: 'Palette' },
            { value: 'fixed', label: 'Fixed color' },
          ],
        },
      });
  })
  // Declare per-field overrides (shown in the Overrides tab)
  .useFieldConfig({
    standardOptions: {
      color: { fixedColorMode: 'fixed' },
    },
    useCustomConfig: (builder) => {
      builder.addNumberInput({
        path: 'lineWidth',
        name: 'Line width',
        defaultValue: 1,
      });
    },
  })
  // Handle migration from a previous panel type
  .setPanelChangeHandler((panel, prevPluginId, prevOptions) => {
    if (prevPluginId === 'graph') {
      return { ...panel, options: { showLegend: true } };
    }
    return panel;
  })
  // Declare what data features the panel supports
  .setDataSupport({ annotations: false, alertStates: false });
```

### 7.4 `MyPanel.tsx` — The React Component

```tsx
import React from 'react';
import { PanelProps } from '@grafana/data';
import { Options } from './types';

// PanelProps<Options> provides: data, options, width, height, timeRange,
// timeZone, onOptionsChange, onFieldConfigChange, replaceVariables, …
export function MyPanel({ data, options, width, height }: PanelProps<Options>) {
  const frame = data.series[0];

  if (!frame) {
    return <div>No data</div>;
  }

  return (
    <div style={{ width, height, overflow: 'auto' }}>
      {options.showLegend && <div className="legend">Legend</div>}
      {/* render your visualization here */}
    </div>
  );
}
```

### 7.5 `types.ts` — Options and FieldConfig

```ts
// Panel-level options (one set per panel instance)
export interface Options {
  showLegend: boolean;
  colorMode: 'palette' | 'fixed';
}

// Per-field config (can be overridden per series/field)
export interface FieldConfig {
  lineWidth?: number;
}
```

### 7.6 Registering the Plugin with the Build System

Built-in panel plugins are automatically discovered by Webpack because `public/app/plugins/panel/` is included in the Yarn workspace glob:

```json
// package.json (root)
"workspaces": {
  "packages": [
    "public/app/plugins/*/*"   // ← picks up your new panel
  ]
}
```

No additional registration step is needed for built-in panels. The plugin loader resolves `module.tsx` at runtime via SystemJS using the plugin ID from `plugin.json`.

For **external** plugins (distributed separately), you would instead use the `@grafana/create-plugin` scaffolding tool and the `@grafana/plugin-configs` Webpack preset from `packages/grafana-plugin-configs/`.

### 7.7 Testing Your Panel

```bash
# Unit tests — create MyPanel.test.tsx alongside MyPanel.tsx
yarn test --testPathPattern=my-panel

# Start the dev server and open Grafana at http://localhost:3000
# Add a new panel to a dashboard and select "My Panel" from the panel picker
yarn start
```

### 7.8 Key `PanelPlugin` Builder Methods Reference

| Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `setPanelOptions(builder => …)` | Declare panel-level option editors |
| `useFieldConfig({ standardOptions, useCustomConfig })` | Declare per-field config editors |
| `setPanelChangeHandler(fn)` | Migrate options when switching from another panel type |
| `setDataSupport({ annotations, alertStates })` | Opt in to annotations/alert state overlays |
| `setSuggestionsSupplier(supplier)` | Provide auto-suggestions in the panel picker |
| `setNoPadding()` | Remove the default panel padding (useful for map/canvas panels) |
| `setSkipDataQuery()` | Panel does not query data (e.g., text, image panels) |

# Grafana v11.4.0 — Engineering Onboarding Guide

This document is a practical orientation for engineers joining the Grafana codebase. It covers the six areas you need to understand before making your first meaningful contribution: server bootstrap, core backend packages, datasource plugin architecture, dashboard provisioning, the frontend build system, and how to add a new panel plugin.
